query: AJCC TNM classification guidelines for head and neck cancers

retrieved_docs.title: Hypopharyngeal Cancer -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor-node-metastasis (TNM) staging, developed by the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) and the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC), is the standard system used to describe the progression of hypopharyngeal cancer. The latest version is the 8th edition, released in 2018 (see Image . Hypopharyngeal Cancer Staging). [46] According to the AJCC UICC TNM 8th edition, hypopharyngeal cancers are assigned a clinical stage as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Oropharyngeal Squamous Cell Carcinoma -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: The American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) has defined the staging of oropharyngeal cancer. This follows a TNM staging system where T relates to the extent of the primary tumor, N indicates the involvement of regional lymph nodes, and M specifies the distant metastasis. In their 8th edition of the staging manual, AJCC has modified the staging system being used for staging oropharyngeal cancer with the addition of depth of invasion (DOI) of the primary tumor as a modification to the T category and extranodal extension (ENE) to upstage node-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma [18] . The new version of classification is more complex than its previous one, but it is better in terms of stratifying survival of oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma patients by stage [19] .

retrieved_docs.title: Tonsil Cancer -- Staging -- M Classification
retrieved_docs.content: M0: No distant metastasis M1: Distant metastasis

retrieved_docs.title: TNM Classification -- Issues of Concern -- Population-Based Versus Personalized Cancer Staging
retrieved_docs.content: While the TNM system is useful as a classification system for carcinoma on a population level, its utility has been questioned on the individual patient level. Therefore, more personalized approaches have been suggested, including adding a molecular classification to the traditional approach to augment its overall utility. The Eighth AJCC Cancer Staging Manual has taken the early steps of acknowledging the importance of molecular oncology. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Melanoma of the Head and Neck -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Staging is carefully performed utilizing the most up-to-date American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) Eighth Edition Guidelines for Melanoma. Important factors determining the stage of cancer and communicating this information are TMN criteria (T, tumor mass; N, lymph nodes; M, metastases).

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer of the Oral Mucosa -- Staging -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor, node, and metastasis (TNM) staging is crucial for categorizing tumors, predicting prognosis, and devising treatment plans. This staging system integrates clinical, histological, and radiological assessments, focusing on the primary tumor, neck lymph nodes, and distant metastases. In 2017, The American Joint Committee on Cancer published changes in the 8th edition of the Cancer Staging Manual on TNM classification for oral cavity and oropharyngeal cancers, aiming to improve differentiation between these 2 cancer types. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Parotidectomy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The TNM staging system is frequently used for major salivary gland tumors (T describes the tumor's size and any cancer spread into nearby tissue; N describes the spread of cancer to nearby lymph nodes; and M describes metastasis. Taro et al analyzed data from 4520 patients in the United States National Cancer Database to create an adapted lymph node staging system for salivary gland tumors. Mortality increased rapidly for every lymph node below 4 and at a slower pattern above 4. The staging system can also be criticized for encompassing all types of salivary gland tumors, while obviously, each histologic subtype behaves differently. The standard of care staging system remains the American Joint Committee on Cancer, AJCC, 8th edition as of the date of this article. [25] Types of parotid surgery include:

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Malignant Melanoma: Guideline-Based Management and Interprofessional Collaboration -- Function -- American Joint Committee on Cancer, AJCC, eighth edition, TNM staging of cutaneous malignant melanoma:
retrieved_docs.content: Cutaneous malignant melanoma is further grouped into clinical and pathological prognostic stages using the information from T, N, and M classification. Clinical staging includes information obtained through clinical and radiological assessment, including from tumor biopsy. Information from further pathological assessment such as wide local tumor excision, sentinel lymph node biopsy, and completion lymph node dissection is used for pathological staging. Stage grouping provides prognostic information with an estimated five-year survival of stage I, II, III as 97 to 99%,75 to 88%,24 to 88%, respectively. Tables 4 and 5 lists the pathological and clinical prognostic staging of the cutaneous malignant melanoma per AJCC eighth edition.

retrieved_docs.title: Glottic Cancer -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: The staging of glottic and other laryngeal cancers is according to the 2016 eighth edition of the AJCC TNM classification of malignant tumors. Unlike oral and oropharyngeal cancers, depth of invasion and p16 status does not have a role in staging glottic tumors. The extra-nodal extension is one of the most important predictors of clinical outcomes in head and neck cancers. To reflect this, TNM8 introduced the presence of extra-nodal extension into all laryngeal cancer staging, with its presence immediately classifying a patient as N3b regardless of nodal size, quantity, or location; to accurately reflect the negative impact this finding can have on prognosis. [48] The TMN8 Tumour (T) staging is as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma (NPC, Lymphoepithelioma) -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: The nuances in imaging techniques and the improved outcomes associated with optimum therapy have caused the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) to reevaluate the staging process. This tumor is stagged according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC), and the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) tumor, node, metastasis (TNM) system. The 7th edition of the UICC/JNCC staging system was replaced by the 8th edition (last update) in 2016. As per the last update of classification, the TNM staging has been explained as the following criteria (Table 1 summarize the staging groups, Table 2 summarize the UICC/AJCC 8th edition staging of NPC): [5] See Tables. retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Malignant Melanoma: Guideline-Based Management and Interprofessional Collaboration -- Function -- American Joint Committee on Cancer, AJCC, eighth edition, TNM staging of cutaneous malignant melanoma:
retrieved_docs.content: M category, which designates distant metastases, is further stratified based on the site of metastases. At the same time, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) level is indicated as a descriptor for each M category for its prognostic value. Table 3 below describes the M category classification.

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Malignant Melanoma: Guideline-Based Management and Interprofessional Collaboration -- Function -- American Joint Committee on Cancer, AJCC, eighth edition, TNM staging of cutaneous malignant melanoma:
retrieved_docs.content: Satellite metastases include clinically evident cutaneous or subcutaneous metastases occurring discontinuous from and less than 2cm from the primary tumor, whereas micro-satellite and satellite metastases refer to similar lesions but clinically non-evident and only detected microscopically. In-transit metastases include metastases discontinuous from and more than 2cm from the primary tumor. Two or more nodes adherent to one another detected are classified as matted nodes.  N categories are further subcategorized using descriptors for clinically occult (N1a, N2a, N3a), clinically apparent (N1b, N2b, N3b), and non-nodal locoregional metastases (N1c, N2c, N3c). Table 2 below describes the N category classification.

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Malignant Melanoma: Guideline-Based Management and Interprofessional Collaboration -- Function -- American Joint Committee on Cancer, AJCC, eighth edition, TNM staging of cutaneous malignant melanoma:
retrieved_docs.content: The tumor category is determined based on Breslow tumor thickness and the presence or absence of ulceration. Measurement of Breslow tumor thickness to 0.1mm is recommended, round down to those ending in decimal places 1 to 4, and round up to decimal places ending from 5 to 9. Ulceration is determined based on histopathological examination and is defined as the full-thickness absence of epidermis above any portion of the primary tumor. The absence of the epidermis due to biopsy-related trauma should not be designated as ulceration. There may be an associated host reaction to ulceration; when there is doubt if the origin of ulceration is tumor-related versus iatrogenic, then the tumor is staged as ulcerated. Mitotic rate is no longer a T category criterion even though it is thought to have prognostic significance, and assessment of mitotic rate is recommended. Table 1 below describes the T category classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1154
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is classified as a type of الورم السرطاني (malignant tumor) and its associated morphology is described by الورم النقيلي (metastatic cancer). This indicates that the spread of carcinoma to the lymph nodes in the head and neck region represents a malignant neoplasm whose pathological features correspond to metastatic disease, a distinction that is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2183
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck represents a specific manifestation of سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق (squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck). This relationship indicates that the metastatic disease involving cervical lymph nodes is a subset of the broader category of head‑and‑neck squamous cell carcinoma, emphasizing the importance of evaluating nodal involvement for accurate staging, prognosis, and treatment planning in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2667
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node is recognized as a manifestation of head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق). This relationship indicates that the spread of malignant cells to the lymph nodes within the head and neck region is a specific form of head and neck cancer, highlighting its relevance for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head refers to any cancer that has spread to structures within the head region, and within this broader category the specific condition of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head represents a more precise manifestation. Recognizing that metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head is a subset of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head helps clinicians assess the extent of disease spread, determine appropriate staging, and tailor management strategies for patients with head‑region metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is clinically associated with head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق), indicating that smoking is a significant risk factor for the development of malignancies in the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and related structures. Head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق) can manifest as metastasis to head and neck lymph nodes (Metastasis to head and neck lymph node), reflecting a common pattern of regional spread for these tumors. Together, these concepts underscore the importance of tobacco cessation in preventing head and neck cancer and the need for careful evaluation of cervical lymph nodes when diagnosing and staging the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2502
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the neck is classified as a secondary malignancy, reflecting the spread of a primary cancer to regional lymphatic tissue. Recognizing this metastatic involvement as a secondary malignancy is important for accurate staging, prognosis, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2395
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to head is a broad category of cancer spread that encompasses more specific manifestations such as metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of head. The latter represents a distinct subset of head metastases, focusing on involvement of the regional lymphatic structures. Because metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of head reflects disease that has spread from a primary site, it is classified under secondary malignancies, the overarching term for cancers that arise as metastases rather than as primary tumors. Recognizing that lymph node involvement in the head is both a specific form of head metastasis and a type of secondary malignancy helps clinicians accurately stage disease, guide treatment planning, and communicate prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1112
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck, Metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of head and neck, and Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck are all classified as secondary malignancies. The broader category نقائل إلى الرقبة encompasses Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck, indicating an inverse hierarchical relationship. In terms of morphological description, Metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is associated with the morphology termed سرطانة غدية نقيلية, while Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck corresponds to the morphology described as السرطان النقيلي. These relationships highlight that metastatic involvement of cervical and head‑and‑neck lymph nodes represents secondary cancer spread, with specific histopathologic subtypes identified by both English and Arabic terminology, which is important for accurate diagnosis, coding, and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2340
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node and metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck are both classified as secondary malignancies, reflecting the spread of a primary cancer to regional lymphatic tissue in the head and neck region. In addition, metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is directly linked to the morphological pattern described by the Arabic term “السرطان النقيلي,” which denotes metastatic cancer. Recognizing these entities as secondary malignancies and understanding their characteristic metastatic morphology are important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management of patients with head‑and‑neck cancer spread.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2567
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck is recognized as a specific subtype of سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق (head and neck squamous cell carcinoma). This metastatic disease displays the characteristic morphology associated with سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية (metastatic squamous cell carcinoma), indicating that the pathological features of the tumor in the lymph nodes reflect the typical patterns of squamous cell carcinoma spread. Understanding that this condition represents an advanced, morphologically distinct form of head and neck squamous cell carcinoma is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck is classified as a specific form of نقائل إلى الرقبة (neck metastases), indicating that this particular cancer spread to the cervical lymph nodes falls under the broader category of metastases to the neck. This relationship underscores the importance of recognizing cervical lymph‑node involvement as a distinct manifestation of neck metastasis in oncologic evaluation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1931
retrieved_docs.content: Head and Neck Surgery is a medical specialty that includes procedures such as بضع طبلة الأذن, which is classified as a type of Head and Neck Surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1040
retrieved_docs.content: The metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the neck that originates from a primary malignant neoplasm of the thyroid, the primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone, and the entity described as “سرطانة توتية” are all linked to the concept of a primary malignant tumor (ورم خبيث أولي). Both the thyroid‑derived cervical lymph‑node metastasis and the nasal‑bone primary tumor are noted as having an associated morphology of a primary malignant tumor, indicating that they share characteristic pathological features of primary malignancies. The inverse_isa relationship further classifies “سرطانة توتية” as a type of primary malignant tumor. Together, these relationships illustrate that diverse tumor presentations—whether metastatic from the thyroid to neck lymph nodes, arising primarily in the nasal bone, or identified as “سرطانة توتية”—are encompassed within the broader category of primary malignant neoplasms, a classification that is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2551
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is categorized as an اضطراب صماوي (endocrine disorder) and is anatomically associated with head and neck structures. This connection highlights that thyroid malignancies arise within the endocrine system and involve the anatomical region of the head and neck, which is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and targeted patient care.
